channel.title: # 🇱🇰 Sri Lanka This Week
channel.description: Generated by gpt-5 from 182 English News Articles published between 2026-02-24 and 2026-03-03.

item.title: Arrest of ex-intelligence chief jolts Easter probe while Church urges restraint
item.description: The Criminal Investigation Department arrested former State Intelligence Service (SIS) Director Maj-Gen (Retd.) Suresh Sallay under the Prevention of Terrorism Act (PTA) over the 2019 Easter Sunday bombings. Police called it a “major breakthrough,” though charges have not yet been detailed. Colombo’s Archdiocese welcomed progress but urged all actors not to politicise the case or obstruct due process. The move revives long-dormant leads, including contested claims aired by Channel 4 in 2023, and tests the Government’s pledge to pursue masterminds impartially. The credibility of the probe now hinges on transparent court proceedings, evidence-led indictments and protection of suspects’ rights—elements essential to building public trust after seven years of delay.

item.title: Middle East escalation threatens Sri Lanka’s recovery through oil, remittances and aviation
item.description: Missile and air strikes involving Israel, the US and Iran triggered rapid airspace closures across the Gulf, suspending or rerouting traffic through the region’s hub airports. SriLankan Airlines cancelled multiple flights to Dubai, Doha, Riyadh, Kuwait and Dammam, citing safety, while global oil prices jumped on fears over the Strait of Hormuz. Analysts warn of spillovers: higher fuel and freight costs, weaker European tourism flows via Gulf hubs, and potential pressure on remittances if Gulf employment softens. Government spokesmen called for de-escalation and said missions were on standby; no mass repatriation is feasible while airports are shut. The prudent course is scenario planning—fuel hedging discipline, contingency air-routing, liquidity buffers for tourism firms—and avoiding domestic polarisation over a foreign conflict. ✈️🛢️

item.title: Inflation dips to 1.6% but targeting debate heats up as CBSL keeps missing
item.description: Colombo Consumer Price Index (CCPI) headline inflation eased to 1.6% YoY in February, with food near flat, but core at 2.1%. The Central Bank of Sri Lanka (CBSL) still reports persistent undershoots versus the 5% target (±2pp), filing repeated deviation reports to Parliament. Former Deputy Governor W.A. Wijewardena argues the target should be reset to around 2%, closer to estimated productivity, warning that a too-high target weakens credibility; CBSL has stressed inflation should gradually move to 5% in H2 2026. Meanwhile, the external accounts started 2026 strongly: a current account surplus of $369.7m in January, exports up 9.1% YoY and remittances up 31%. Gross reserves rose, and the rupee was marginally firmer YTD—tailwinds that will reverse fast if oil spikes or air routes remain impaired.

item.title: Power sector enters a fraught week: strikes, tariffs and coal quality collide
item.description: CEB engineers and allied unions staged a six-hour token strike over restructuring, with warnings of escalation if six subsidiary entities are gazetted without agreed safeguards. A collective of over 2,000 staff who applied for the Voluntary Retirement Scheme backed restructuring and urged action under the Essential Public Services Act against illegal stoppages. In parallel, the Norochcholai plant reported output down to about 750–760 MW from 810 MW when burning South African coal supplied under the 2025/26 tender; independent lab tests say specs are met except for the first shipment, prompting the Energy Ministry to appoint a technical committee and add a third-party load-port surveyor. The Public Utilities Commission (PUCSL) has opened consultations on tariff revisions even as fuel and forex risks rise. The policy imperative remains clear: accelerate least-cost renewables and grid upgrades, ring-fence lifeline consumers, and enforce discipline across the value chain to avoid blackouts and protect solvency. ⚡

item.title: Banks fatten profits as markets stabilise; exporters eye margin over volume
item.description: Listed lenders reported robust 2025 earnings—HNB Group PAT LKR 49.8 bn, People’s Bank standalone PAT LKR 40.2 bn, Sampath PAT LKR 32.6 bn, Seylan PAT LKR 12.1 bn—on lower impairments, balance‑sheet growth and improved asset quality. The Colombo bourse held up despite bouts of risk-off trade tied to tariff and Gulf headlines. January’s external data showed a narrower trade deficit and strong remittances; February apparel exports softened 2.7% YoY, but Sri Lanka’s zero-tariff access to the UK under the DCTS and recent US tariff resets offer respite if firms pivot to higher-yield niches. Japanese FDI sentiment improved: over half of JETRO‑surveyed firms plan expansion in Sri Lanka over two years, though profitability and policy clarity remain watchpoints.

item.title: ‘Himikama’ land titles could unlock rural capital if backed by cadastre and credit
item.description: The Government began rolling out ‘Himikama’ freehold deeds in Anuradhapura, converting long‑held permits under the Land Development Ordinance into full title on voluntary surrender. The policy aims to turn land into bankable collateral and catalyse on‑farm investment, while reserving areas for food security. International evidence suggests titling works when coupled with clear surveying, dispute resolution, and financial inclusion; without these, titles risk distress sales or speculative concentration. To turn paper into productivity, the State needs to complete cadastral maps, digitise registries, and expand extension and risk‑sharing credit for smallholders. 📜🌾

item.title: Tourism’s near-term turbulence tests logistics, not demand
item.description: The Gulf airspace shutdown created an operational shock for Sri Lanka-bound long‑haul passengers who rely on Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi. Hotels reported short‑term disruptions and airport‑area spillovers, while SriLankan and foreign carriers worked on reroutes via Istanbul and other corridors. The underlying demand trend remains supportive after a strong start to 2026; the risk lies in prolonged hub closures or oil above $90–100, which would lift fares and dampen bookings. Policy should focus on diversified air links, visa facilitation, and clear messaging that Sri Lanka is open and safe—even as the country builds a more resilient, higher‑yield tourism mix.
